blocks:
Ayahs

لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (١)
I swear by the Day of Resurrection! (1)
وَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلنَّفْسِ ٱللَّوَّامَةِ (٢)
And I swear by the self-critical soul! (2)
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَلَّن نَّجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُۥ (٣)
Does man suppose that We will not put together his bones [at resurrection]? (3)
بَلَىٰ قَـٰدِرِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّسَوِّىَ بَنَانَهُۥ (٤)
Yes indeed, We are able to [re]shape [even] his fingertips! (4)
بَلْ يُرِيدُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ (٥)
Indeed, man desires to go on living viciously. (5)
يَسْـَٔلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (٦)
He asks, ‘When will be this “day of resurrection”?!’ (6)

Content of Surah Al-Qiyamah

As is evident from the name of the Surah, its discussions revolve around matters related to the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, except for a few verses that speak about the "Glorious Quran" and those who deny it. The discussions regarding the Resurrection in this Surah can be categorized into four main themes:

  1. Matters related to the "Signs of the Hour" (the extraordinary and terrifying events that occur at the end of this world and the beginning of the Resurrection).

  2. Matters related to the state of the righteous and the wicked on that Day.

  3. Matters related to the anxiety-filled moments of death and the transition from this world to the next.

  4. Discussions regarding the purpose of human creation and its relationship with the issue of the Resurrection.

Virtue of Reciting the Surah

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Qiyamah, I and Jibra’il will bear witness for him on the Day of Resurrection that he believed in that Day, and he will come forth with his face more radiant than the faces of other creatures on the Day of Judgment." ^1

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 393.

And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever is constant in reciting 'La Uqsimu' (Surah Al-Qiyamah) and acts upon it, Allah will raise this Surah with him from his grave on the Day of Resurrection in the most beautiful form, giving him glad tidings and smiling in his face, until he passes the Sirat and the Balance." ^1

It is noteworthy that what we used to infer from clues regarding the virtue of reciting Quranic Surahs in other instances is explicitly stated here in the text of the narration; for it says: "Whoever is constant in it and acts upon it." Therefore, all of this is a prelude to acting upon and implementing its contents.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 393.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful)

لَا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ (1) وَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِالنَّفْسِ اللَّوَّامَةِ (2) أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَلَّنْ نَجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُ (3) بَلَى قَادِرِينَ عَلَى أَنْ نُسَوِّيَ بَنَانَهُ (4) بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسَانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُ (5) يَسْأَلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ (6)

Commentary

An Oath by the Day of Resurrection and the Reproaching Conscience?

This Surah begins with two profound oaths, stating: "I swear by the Day of Resurrection" (لَا أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ).

"And I swear by the awakening conscience of humans and the reproaching self" (وَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِالنَّفْسِ اللَّوَّامَةِ).

There is a discussion among commentators whether the "La" in these two verses is "redundant" (za'idah) and used for emphasis (thus not negating the oath, but making it more emphatic), or whether "La" is negative, aiming to say that this subject is so important that I do not [need to] swear by it (similar to how people sometimes say to one another, "I won't swear by your life," implying it is beyond an oath).

Most commentators have chosen the first possibility, while some favor the second interpretation, believing that a redundant "La" does not appear at the beginning of a sentence but must be in the middle.

However, the first interpretation seems more correct, because the Quran has sworn by matters even more important than the Resurrection, such as the Pure Essence of Allah; therefore, there is no reason why the Day of Resurrection should not be sworn by here. Furthermore, there are precedents for placing a redundant "La" at the beginning of a sentence, as cited from the poetry of Imru' al-Qais, who used a redundant "La" at the start of some of his odes: La wa abika ibnata al-'Amiri... la yud'a al-qawmu anni afirru ^1

^1: "No, by your father, O daughter of 'Amir, the people shall never claim that I flee."

In our view, however, the discussion regarding whether "La" is redundant or negative is not very important, as the final result of both is the same: the significance of the subject for which the oath was taken.

The important thing is to see what relationship exists between these two oaths (the oath by the Day of Resurrection and the oath by the awakening conscience).

The truth is that one of the proofs for the existence of the "Resurrection" is the existence of the "Court of Conscience" within the human soul, which fills the human spirit with joy and vitality when performing a good deed—thereby rewarding him—and severely pressures, punishes, and tortures his soul when committing an evil act or a crime, to the extent that he sometimes resorts to suicide to escape the agony of conscience.

This means, in reality, the conscience has issued his death sentence and executed it by his own hand! The reflection of the "Reproaching Self" (al-Nafs al-Lawwamah) in human existence is very vast and extensive, worthy of careful study from every perspective, and we will refer to it more in the "Key Points" section.

When the "Microcosm"—that is, human existence—has a small court and tribunal within its heart, how could the "Macrocosm" with all its grandeur not have a Great Court of Justice?

It is from here that we infer the existence of the "Resurrection and Judgment" from the existence of the "Moral Conscience," and from here the beautiful relationship between these two oaths becomes clear. In other words, the second oath is a proof for the first oath.

Regarding what is meant by "al-Nafs al-Lawwamah" ^1, commentators have mentioned various interpretations. One famous interpretation is what we mentioned above: the "moral conscience" that reproaches man during wrongdoings in this world and compels him to compensate and reconsider.

Another interpretation is that it refers to the reproaching of all humans by themselves on the Day of Judgment. The believers reproach themselves as to why they performed so few righteous deeds, and the disbelievers as to why they followed the path of disbelief, polytheism, and sin.

Another is that it refers only to the souls of the disbelievers, which will severely reproach and blame them on the Day of Judgment for their evil deeds.

However, the first interpretation is most consistent with the preceding and following verses. Indeed, this court of conscience has such grandeur and respect that Allah swears by it and holds it in high esteem. It is truly great, for it is considered one of the important factors for human salvation, provided that the conscience remains awake and does not become weak and powerless due to the abundance of sins.

It is also noteworthy that following these two highly significant and meaningful oaths, it is not explicitly stated what the oath was taken for; the "object of the oath" (muqsam lahu) is omitted. This is because the matter is clear from the context of the following verses. Therefore, the above verses mean: "I swear by the Day of Resurrection and the reproaching self that you shall all be resurrected on the Day of Judgment and receive the recompense for your deeds." [^2]

[^2]: The omitted phrase is "la-tub'athunna yawm al-qiyamah" (you shall surely be resurrected on the Day of Judgment) or "innakum tub'athun" (indeed you are to be resurrected).

Interestingly, the Day of Resurrection is sworn by to prove that there is a Resurrection and a Rising. This is because the issue of the Resurrection is considered so certain that it can even be used as an oath against its deniers.

Then, in the next verse, as a rhetorical question, He adds: "Does man think that We shall not assemble his bones?" (أَيَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَلَّنْ نَجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُ).

"Yes, We are able even to restore his fingertips (the lines of his fingertips) to their original, balanced, and orderly state" (بَلَى قَادِرِينَ عَلَى أَنْ نُسَوِّيَ بَنَانَهُ).

It is mentioned in a narration that one of the polytheists who lived in the neighborhood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله named "Ali ibn Abi Rabi'ah" came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked about the Day of Resurrection: how will it be and when will it occur? Then he added: "Even if I were to see that day with my own eyes, I would still not believe you or have faith in you! Is it possible for Allah to gather these bones? This is unbelievable!" It was here that the above verses were revealed and answered him. Therefore, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to say regarding this stubborn and antagonistic man: "O Allah, protect me from the evil of the bad neighbor." ^1 Similar meanings are seen in other verses of the Quran, including verse 78 of Surah Ya-Sin, where we read: one of the deniers of the Resurrection took a piece of decayed bone...

^1: This narration is reported by al-Maraghi, as well as in Ruh al-Ma'ani and Tafsir al-Safi with slight variations.

...in his hand and said to the Prophet: "Who can give life to these bones when they are decayed?" (مَنْ يُحْيِي الْعِظَامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ).

Furthermore, the expression "yahsabu" (from the root husban meaning "to imagine" or "to suppose") indicates that these deniers never truly believed in their own words, but rather relied only on vain and baseless imaginations and assumptions.

But let us see why bones were specifically emphasized. This is because, firstly, the durability of bone is greater than other organs; thus, when it decays, turns to dust, and its particles are scattered, the hope of its return seems less likely to superficial people.

Secondly, bone is the most important pillar of the human body, for bones form the columns of the body, and all movements, displacements, and important activities of the body are performed by means of bones. The abundance, variety, shapes, and different sizes of bones in the human body are considered among the wonders of Allah's creation, and the value of a single small vertebra in a person's back becomes apparent when it fails, as we see it paralyzes the entire body.

"Banan" in the language means both "fingers" and "fingertips." In both cases, it points to the fact that not only does Allah gather the bones and return them to their original state, but He even places the small, delicate, and precise bones of the fingers back in their places, and beyond that, Allah even restores his fingertips in a balanced way to their original form.

This expression can be a subtle reference to the lines on the fingertips of humans, as it is said that hardly any human is found on earth whose fingerprints are identical to...

...another's. In other words, the delicate and complex lines etched on the fingertips of every human are his identity marker. Therefore, in our era, the issue of "fingerprinting" has become a science, through which many criminals are identified and crimes are solved. It is enough, for example, for a thief to place his hand on a door handle, a window pane, or a lock and chest when entering a room or house, leaving the impression of his fingerprints; they immediately take a sample of it, match it with the records they have of thieves and criminals, and find the culprit.

In the next verse, He points to one of the true causes for denying the Resurrection, stating: It is not that man doubts Allah's power to gather bones and revive the dead, "but man desires to continue sinning for the rest of his life" (بَلْ يُرِيدُ الْإِنْسَانُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُ).

He wants, through the denial of the Resurrection, to gain freedom for every kind of self-indulgence, oppression, awakening [of desires], and sin. He wants to both falsely satisfy his conscience this way and not hold himself responsible before Allah's creation, for belief in the Resurrection, the Rising, and the Court of Allah's Justice is a massive barrier against every kind of rebellion and sin. He wants to remove this bridle, break this barrier, and freely perform whatever action he desires.

This was not limited to past times; today as well, one of the causes for the inclination toward materialism and the denial of the Origin and the Resurrection is to gain freedom for "fujur" (wickedness), to escape responsibilities, and to break every divine law; otherwise, the proofs for the Origin...

...and the Resurrection are manifest. In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is stated regarding the explanation of this verse: "He puts sin forward and delays repentance, saying: 'I will repent later.'" The verse refers to someone who prioritizes sin and postpones repentance.

Some have also said that "fujur" in the verse means "denial" (takdhib). Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: man wants to deny the Resurrection and the Rising that lies ahead of him. However, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

And following that, He adds: Therefore, "he asks: 'When will the Day of Resurrection be?'" (يَسْأَلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ). Yes, to escape responsibilities, he asks a rhetorical question about the time of the Resurrection to open the way for his wickedness.

It is necessary to remind that their question about the time of the Resurrection's occurrence does not mean they accepted its essence and were asking about its timing; rather, this question is a prelude to denying the essence of the Resurrection. It is just like someone saying, "So-and-so is coming from a journey," and when it takes a long time and he does not arrive, another person who denies the arrival of that traveler says: "So when will this traveler arrive?"

Key Points

1- "The Court of Conscience" or "The Minor Resurrection"

It is clearly understood from the Glorious Quran that the human soul and self possess three...

...stages:

1- "Al-Nafs al-Ammarah" (The Commanding Self): that is, the rebellious spirit that constantly invites man to ugliness and evil, and beautifies desires and wickedness before him. This is what the wife of the 'Aziz of Egypt, that lustful woman, referred to when she saw the grim end of her work and said: "And I do not acquit myself. Indeed, the soul is a persistent enjoiner of evil" (وَمَا أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ) (Surah Yusuf, verse 53).

2- "Al-Nafs al-Lawwamah" (The Reproaching Self): which is mentioned in the verses under discussion. It is an awake and relatively aware spirit; although it has not yet gained immunity against sin and occasionally slips...

...and falls into the lap of sin, but shortly after, it wakes up, repents, and returns to the path of felicity. Deviation is entirely possible for it, but it is temporary, not permanent. Sin occurs from it, but it does not take long before it gives way to reproach, blame, and repentance.

This is the same thing referred to as "moral conscience." In some humans, it is very strong and powerful, and in some, very weak and feeble, but in any case, it exists in every human unless one completely disables it through an abundance of sins.

3- "Al-Nafs al-Mutma'innah" (The Soul at Peace): that is, the perfected spirit that has reached the stage of certainty, tamed the rebellious self, and reached the station of complete piety and sense of responsibility such that slipping is no longer easily possible for it.

This is what is mentioned in Surah Al-Fajr, verses 27 and 28: "O soul at peace! Return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him]" (يَا أَيَّتُهَا النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ارْجِعِي إِلَى رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَرْضِيَّةً). In any case, this "Reproaching Self," as we said, is a small resurrection within the soul of every human that, immediately after performing a good or bad deed, forms its court within the soul and settles his account.

Therefore, sometimes in the face of a good and important deed, one feels such inner peace, and his soul is so overflowed with joy and vitality, that its pleasure, glory, and beauty cannot be described by any tongue or pen.

Conversely, sometimes following a major transgression or crime, one becomes so trapped in a terrifying nightmare and a storm of grief and sorrow, burning from within, that he becomes completely weary of life, and sometimes even consciously turns himself in to judicial authorities and submits to the gallows to escape the clutches of this distress.

This extraordinary internal court bears a striking resemblance to the Court of Resurrection:

1- The judge, the witness, and the executor of the sentence are in fact one here, just as it is on the Day of Judgment: "O Allah, Knower of the unseen and the seen, You will judge between Your servants" (عَالِمَ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ أَنْتَ تَحْكُمُ بَيْنَ عِبَادِكَ) (Az-Zumar, verse 46).

2- This court of conscience does not accept recommendations, bribes, favoritism, or the common human practice of falsifying files, just as we read regarding the Court of Resurrection: "And fear a Day when no soul will suffice for another soul at all, nor will intercession be accepted from it, nor will compensation be taken from it, nor will they be helped" (وَاتَّقُوا يَوْماً لَا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَنْ نَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَلَا يُقْبَلُ مِنْهَا شَفَاعَةٌ وَلَا يُؤْخَذُ مِنْهَا عَدْلٌ وَلَا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ) (Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 48).

3- The court of conscience examines the most important and thickest files in the shortest...

...time and issues its final verdict swiftly. There is no appeal in it, no reconsideration, and no months or years of wandering, just as we read regarding the Court of Resurrection: "And Allah judges; there is no adjuster of His judgment, and He is swift in account" (وَاللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسَابِ) (Surah Al-Ra'd, verse 41).

4- Its punishment and penalty, unlike the punishments of the formal courts of this world, have their first sparks ignited in the depths of the heart and soul, and spread outward from there. First, it torments the human spirit, then its effects appear in the body, the face, and the disruption of his sleep and food, just as we read regarding the Court of Resurrection: "It is the fire of Allah, [eternally] fueled, which mounts directed at the hearts" (نَارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ) (Surah Al-Humazah, verses 6-7).

5- This court of conscience does not need many observers and witnesses; rather, it accepts the knowledge and awareness of the accused person himself as "witnesses" for or against him! Just as in the Court of Resurrection, the particles of human existence, even his hands, feet, and skin, are witnesses to his deeds, as He says: "Until, when they reach it, their hearing and their eyes and their skins will testify against them" (حَتَّى إِذَا مَا جَاءُوهَا شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَأَبْصَارُهُمْ وَجُلُودُهُمْ) (Surah Fussilat, verse 20).

This extraordinary similarity between these two courts is another sign of the innate (fitari) nature of the issue of the Resurrection. For how can it be believed that in the existence of a human, who is a small drop in this vast ocean of existence, such a mysterious and secretive account and court exists, but within this great universe, there is absolutely no account, book, court, or tribunal? This is unbelievable. ^1

2- Names of the Resurrection in the Glorious Quran

We know that a significant part of Quranic knowledge and its doctrinal issues revolve around matters related to the Resurrection and the Rising, for it has the most important impact on human education and the process of his perfection.

The names chosen in the Quran for this great Day are also many, and each one expresses a dimension of that Day's aspects, and alone can convey many issues in this regard.

As the late "Fayd al-Kashani" said in Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', beneath each of these names lies a secret, and in every description, an important meaning is expressed; one must strive to understand these meanings and find these secrets.

He mentioned more than a hundred names for the Resurrection, all or most of which can be derived from the Glorious Quran. Such as "Yawm al-Hasrah" (Day of Regret), "Yawm al-Nadamah" (Day of Remorse), "Yawm al-Muhasabah" (Day of Accounting), "Yawm al-Mas'alah" (Day of Questioning), "Yawm al-Waqi'ah" (The Inevitable Event), "Yawm al-Qari'ah" (The Striking Calamity), "Yawm al-Rajifah" (The Quaking), "Yawm al-Radifah" (The Following), "Yawm al-Talaq" (Day of Meeting), "Yawm al-Firaq" (Day of Separation), "Yawm al-Hisab" (Day of Account), "Yawm al-Tanad" (Day of Calling Out), "Yawm al-'Adhab" (Day of Punishment), "Yawm al-Firar" (Day of Fleeing), "Yawm al-Haqq" (The Day of Truth), "Yawm al-Hukm" (Day of Judgment), "Yawm al-Fasl" (Day of Sorting), "Yawm al-Jam'" (Day of Gathering), "Yawm al-Din" (Day of Recompense), "Yawm Tubla al-Sara'ir" (The Day when secrets are tested), "The Day when no protector will avail a protector at all," "The Day a man will flee from his brother," "The Day when neither wealth nor children will benefit," and "Yawm al-Taghabun" (Day of Mutual Loss), etc. [^2]

^1: For further explanation regarding the court of conscience, refer to the book Great Leaders (discussion on conscience) and the book Resurrection and the World After Death (discussion on proofs of Resurrection). [^2]: Al-Mahajjat al-Bayda', Vol. 8, p. 331 (abridged).

However, its most famous name is "Yawm al-Qiyamah" (Day of Resurrection), which is mentioned seventy times in the Glorious Quran, and it tells of the general rising of the servants and the great resurrection of humans, and attention to it also invites man to rise in this world to perform his duty.

In our view, to wake up from the sleep of negligence and pride, to bridle the rebellious self, and for the education and training of man, it is sufficient to reflect on these names and consider our situation on that great Day—the Day when we all appear before the presence of the Great Lord, the veils are removed, inner secrets become manifest, Paradise is adorned, and Hell is fueled, and we all appear at the scale of Divine Justice. (O Allah, grant us refuge under Your protection on that Day).

Ayahs

فَإِذَا بَرِقَ ٱلْبَصَرُ (٧)
But when the eyes are dazzled, (7)
وَخَسَفَ ٱلْقَمَرُ (٨)
the moon is eclipsed, (8)
وَجُمِعَ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ (٩)
and the sun and the moon are brought together, (9)
يَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ ٱلْمَفَرُّ (١٠)
that day man will say, ‘Where is the escape [from this day]?’ (10)
كَلَّا لَا وَزَرَ (١١)
No indeed! There will be no refuge! (11)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمُسْتَقَرُّ (١٢)
That day the [final] goal will be toward your Lord. (12)
يُنَبَّؤُا۟ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ (١٣)
That day man will be informed about what [works] he had sent ahead [to the scene of judgement] and [the legacy that he had] left behind. (13)
بَلِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ بَصِيرَةٌ (١٤)
Indeed, man is a witness to himself, (14)
وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُۥ (١٥)
though he should offer excuses [to justify his failings]. (15)

Commentary

Man is His Own Best Judge

In the preceding verses, the discussion led to the question that the deniers of the Resurrection had regarding the Day of Judgment; they would say: "If the Resurrection is true, when will it come?!" The verses under discussion provide an eloquent answer to this question.

First, it refers to the events preceding the Resurrection—that is, the great transformation that will occur in the world and the collapse of its order—stating: "When the eyes roll and become agitated from the intensity of horror and terror" (فَإِذا بَرِقَ الْبَصَرُ) [^1]

"And when the moon is eclipsed and loses its light" (وَ خَسَفَ الْقَمَرُ).

[^1]: "Bariqa" (بَرِقَ) is derived from the root "Barq" (lightning). Originally, it means the light and lightning that appear amidst clouds; subsequently, it was applied to any type of light. The "striking of the eyes" mentioned in this verse refers to their rapid and agitated movement due to intense horror and fear. Some have also interpreted it as the fixing of the pupils and staring blankly at a single point, which is also often a sign of terror, citing evidence from Arabic poetry where the striking of the eye means bewilderment. However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

"And when the sun and the moon are joined together" (وَ جُمِعَ الشَّمْسُ وَ الْقَمَرُ).

Regarding what is meant by the "joining of the sun and the moon," commentators have mentioned various interpretations.

Sometimes they have said: both will be placed side by side, or both will rise together from the east and set in the west.

And sometimes they have said: both will be joined in the quality of losing their light [^1]. The possibility also exists that the moon will gradually be drawn toward the sun under the influence of its gravity and eventually be absorbed by it and joined to it, and both will become lightless.

In any case, reference is made here to two of the most important revolutionary phenomena of the end of the world: the moon losing its light and the joining of the sun and the moon together. Similar references are found elsewhere in the Quran; for instance, in verse 1 of Surah At-Takwir, He says: إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ: "When the sun is darkened." And we know that the moon's light is from the sun; when the sun is darkened, the moon also becomes dark, and as a result, the earth will be plunged into a terrifying darkness.

[^1]: The late Tabarsi says in Majma' al-Bayan: "Joining is of three types: joining in place, joining in time, and the joining of qualities in a single thing (like the joining of knowledge and justice in one person). However, joining in the sense of 'two things sharing a quality'—such as the sun and moon both losing their light—is a type of metaphorical expression (which must be understood through context)." (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 395).

In this way, with a great revolution and transformation, the world ends. Then, with another great transformation (with the second blowing of the Trumpet, which is the breath of life), the resurrection of mankind begins, and on that day, man will say: "Where is the place of escape?" (يَقُولُ الْإِنْسانُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ الْمَفَرُّ) [^1]. Yes, the disbelieving and sinful humans who used to deny the Day of Judgment will seek a refuge on that day out of intense shame and embarrassment, and they will seek a way to escape from the heavy burden of sin and the fear of punishment. Just as in this world, when they faced a dangerous event, they would look for a way to escape, they compare that place to this one!

But soon it will be said to them: "By no means! There is no place of escape or refuge here" (كَلَّا لا وَزَرَ) [^2].

"Rather, the final destination for everyone is toward your Lord," and there is no refuge besides Him (إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمُسْتَقَرُّ). Besides the above interpretation, other interpretations have been mentioned for this verse, including: on that day, the final judgment is in the hand of Allah.

[^1]: "Mafar" (مَفَرّ) is a noun of place derived from "Farar" (escape). Some have suggested it might be an infinitive, but this possibility seems unlikely here.

[^2]: "Wazar" (وَزَر) originally means mountain refuges and the like. A "Wazir" (minister) is called so because people take refuge in him for their affairs. In any case, in the verse under discussion, it means any kind of refuge.

Or that the final resting place, whether Paradise or Hell, is by His command.

Or that the standing for trial and accounting is before Him.

However, considering the following verse, the interpretation we chose is the most appropriate of all.

Some believe that this verse is among those that describe the path of man's eternal evolutionary journey, and is in the category of verses that say: وَ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ "And to Him is the final return" (At-Taghabun: 3)—or: يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ: "O man! Verily you are toiling toward your Lord with a [constant] striving, and you will meet Him" (Al-Inshiqaq: 6)—and: وَ أَنَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى‌: "And that to your Lord is the final goal" (An-Najm: 42) [^1]. In clearer terms, humans are wayfarers who have moved from the border of non-existence and have traveled all this way to the realm of existence, and in this realm, they are moving toward Absolute Existence and the infinite being of Allah. Whenever they do not deviate from the original path and the Straight Path, this evolutionary movement will continue forever, and every day they enter a new stage of proximity to Allah. But if they deviate from their path, they will fall and be destroyed.

Then, continuing this discourse, He adds: "On that day, man will be informed of all the deeds he 'sent forward' or 'held back'" (يُنَبَّؤُا الْإِنْسانُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بِما قَدَّمَ وَ أَخَّرَ). Regarding what is meant by these two expressions, many interpretations have been mentioned:

[^1]: Of course, there are other views regarding the interpretation of these verses which we have explained under them.

First, it refers to the deeds he sent forward during his life, or the effects that remained after his death—whether a good or bad tradition he left among the people which they act upon, and its rewards or sins reach him; or books and writings, and charitable or evil buildings, and righteous or unrighteous children, whose effects reach him.

Another is that it refers to the first deeds he performed and the last deeds he did in his life; in other words, he is informed of all his deeds.

Another is the wealth he sent forward (in charity) and the wealth he left behind for heirs.

Some have also said it refers to the sins he prioritized and the acts of obedience he delayed, or vice versa.

But the most appropriate of all is the first interpretation, especially since it is mentioned in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse that he said:

"(He will be informed) of the good and evil he sent forward, and what he left behind of a tradition that is followed after him. If it was evil, he will bear a burden like the burden of those who acted upon it, without it diminishing anything from their burdens. And if it was good, he will have a reward like their rewards, without it diminishing anything from their rewards." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 406. A similar Hadith is also found in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6891.

In the next verse, He adds: Although Allah and His angels inform man of all his deeds, there is no need for this announcement, "Rather, man himself is aware of his own condition," and he and his limbs are witnesses and evidence against him on that Great Day (بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ).

"Even though he may offer his excuses outwardly" (وَ لَوْ أَلْقى‌ مَعاذِيرَهُ‌). These verses, in fact, say the same thing that is mentioned in other verses of the Quran regarding the testimony of man's limbs against his deeds, such as verse 20 of Surah Fussilat which says: شَهِدَ عَلَيْهِمْ سَمْعُهُمْ وَ أَبْصارُهُمْ وَ جُلُودُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ: "Their hearing, their sight, and their skins will bear witness against them as to what they used to do." And verse 65 of Ya-Sin which says: وَ تُكَلِّمُنا أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ تَشْهَدُ أَرْجُلُهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ: "Their hands will speak to Us, and their feet will bear witness to what they used to earn!" Therefore, in that great court of the Resurrection, the best witness to man's deeds is himself, for he knows his own condition better than anyone else, even though Allah has appointed many other witnesses for him to complete the proof.

"Basirah" (بَصِيرَةٌ) has both an infinitive meaning (insight and awareness) and an adjectival meaning (an aware person), and thus some have interpreted it as "proof, evidence, and argument" that provides awareness [^1].

[^1]: According to the first possibility, the "ta" (ة) is for the infinitive; according to the second, it is the feminine "ta" because "insan" (man) here refers to "jawarirh" (limbs) or "nafs" (soul), which are grammatically feminine; some also consider it the "ta of emphasis" (mubalaghah), indicating the intensity of man's awareness of himself.

"Ma'adhir" (مَعاذِير) is the plural of "ma'dhirah" (excuse), originally meaning finding something that removes the traces of sin, which is sometimes a real excuse and sometimes formal and outward. Some have also said "Ma'adhir" is the plural of "mi'dhar," meaning a veil or covering. According to this interpretation, the meaning of the verse becomes: man has awareness of himself even if he casts veils over his deeds and keeps his actions hidden. However, the first interpretation is more appropriate.

In any case, the Judge over the accounting and recompense on that Great Day is One who is aware of both inner and outer secrets, and He makes man himself the accountant of his own deeds, just as it is stated in verse 14 of Surah al-Isra: اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى‌ بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً "Read your record. Sufficient is yourself today as an accountant against you!" Although the verses under discussion all speak about the Hereafter and the Resurrection, their concept is broad and includes the world of this life as well. Here too, people are aware of their own state, even though a group hides their true face with lies, deception, outward show, and hypocrisy.

Therefore, it is mentioned in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said:

"What can any of you do by manifesting good while concealing evil? Is it not that when he returns to himself, he knows it is not so? And Allah, the Sublime, says: 'Rather, man is aware of himself.' Indeed, when the inner self is rectified, the outward becomes strong." [^1]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 396.

And in the Hadiths regarding the fasting of a sick person, it is mentioned that one of the companions of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام asked: "What is the limit of the illness for which its owner may break the fast?" The Imam replied: بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى‌ نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ، هو اعلم بما يطيق: "Man is more aware of his own condition and knows best what he can endure." [^1]

[^1]: Same source (this Hadith was also cited by the late Saduq in Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih in the Book of Fasting—Vol. 2, p. 132, Chapter on the Limit of Illness for which its Owner Breaks the Fast, Hadith 1941).

Ayahs

لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِۦ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِۦٓ (١٦)
Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. (16)
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ (١٧)
Indeed it is up to Us to put it together and to recite it. (17)
فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَـٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ (١٨)
And when We have recited it, follow its recitation. (18)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُۥ (١٩)
Then, its exposition [also] lies with Us. (19)

Commentary

The Collection and Preservation of the Quran is Our Responsibility!

These verses are, in fact, in the nature of a parenthetical statement that a speaker sometimes introduces in the middle of their speech. For example, a person delivering a speech notices that the back of the assembly is crowded while the front is empty; they temporarily interrupt their remarks to invite the audience to move forward to make room for newcomers, and then resume their speech. Or, a teacher, while lecturing, notices a lapse in a student's attention; they pause their discourse to warn the student and then continue the lesson.

If an uninformed person were to listen to a recording of that speech or lecture, they might fall into error and wonder at the lack of connection between these sentences and the preceding or following ones. However, by carefully considering the specific circumstances of the gathering, the philosophy behind these parenthetical statements becomes clear.

With this simple introduction in mind, we turn to the commentary of the verses under discussion.

Allah temporarily leaves the thread of discourse regarding the Day of Resurrection and the states of the believers and disbelievers, and gives a concise reminder to His Prophet regarding the Quran, saying:

"Move not your tongue with it to make haste therewith" (لا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ).

Regarding the commentary of this verse, commentators have much to say, and overall, three interpretations have been mentioned for it.

The first is the well-known interpretation narrated from Ibn Abbas in books of Hadith and Tafsir. It states that because of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله intense love and passion for receiving and memorizing the Quran, when the messenger of revelation would recite the verses to him, he would move his tongue along with him and hasten. Allah forbade him from doing this, saying: "Do not do this; We Ourselves shall collect it for you."

The second is that we know the Quran has two types of revelation: "Instantaneous Revelation," meaning all of it was revealed at once upon the pure heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله on the "Night of Power" (Laylat al-Qadr), and "Gradual Revelation," which took place over 23 years. Because of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله haste in conveying the call, he would sometimes recite the verses before or during their gradual revelation. He was commanded to refrain from this and to let each verse be recited and conveyed at its proper time.

In this way, the theme of this verse is similar to verse 114 of Surah Ta-Ha: (وَ لا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُقْضى‌ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُ) "And hasten not with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you."

These two interpretations do not differ greatly and collectively return to the meaning that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should not hasten even in receiving the revelation.

The third interpretation, which has very few supporters, is that the addressees in these verses are the sinners on the Day of Resurrection who are commanded to read their book of deeds and be their own accountants. They are told: "Do not hasten in reading it." Naturally, when they reach their "evil deeds" while reading their record, they become distressed and want to pass over them quickly. This command is given to them, restraining them from haste, and they are required to follow along as the divine angels read their book of deeds.

According to this interpretation, these verses do not take the form of a parenthetical statement and are related to the preceding and following verses, all being linked together as they all pertain to the states of the Day of Resurrection and the Hereafter. However, according to the first and second interpretations—as we have said—these verses have a parenthetical aspect.

In any case, the third interpretation seems very unlikely, especially considering the mention of the name "Quran" in the subsequent verses. Fundamentally, the tone of the verses clearly indicates that one of the first two interpretations is intended, and there is no obstacle to combining the two, although the tone of the following verses aligns more with the first—the well-known—interpretation (reflect carefully).

Then He adds: "It is for Us to collect it and to recite it" (إِنَّ عَلَيْنا جَمْعَهُ وَ قُرْآنَهُ) [^1]. In short, do not worry about the collection of the Quran; We shall collect its verses and recite them to you again through the messenger of revelation.

"And when We have recited it, then follow its recitation" (فَإِذا قَرَأْناهُ فَاتَّبِعْ قُرْآنَهُ).

"Then it is for Us to make it clear" (ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنا بَيانَهُ). Therefore, the collection of the Quran, its recitation to you, and the explanation and detailing of its meanings are all three Our responsibility. Do not worry at all; He who sent down this revelation is its protector in all stages. Your only duty, on one hand, is to follow the recitation of the messenger of revelation, and on the other, to convey this message to the general public.

Some have also said that by "collection," it does not mean collection in the language of revelation, but rather collection in the chest of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and its recitation upon his tongue. That is: "Do not hasten; We will collect all these verses in your chest and then cause their recitation to flow upon your tongue."

In any case, these expressions all support the first interpretation: that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, during the descent of revelation through Gabriel, was constantly in a hurry to repeat the verses quickly lest they escape his memory. It was here that he was informed by Allah to maintain his composure, and he was assured that not only the collection of the verses and their recitation but also their explanation is guaranteed by Allah.

These verses also signify the authenticity of the Quran and its preservation from any kind of alteration or change, as Allah has promised its collection, recitation, and explanation.

It is mentioned in a Hadith that after the revelation of these verses, whenever Gabriel descended upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, he would remain completely silent, and when Gabriel left, he would begin to recite the verses [^1].

[^1]: Note that "Quran" in this verse and the following verse has a gerundial meaning and means "recitation." [^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 397.

Ayahs

كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ (٢٠)
No! Indeed, you love this transitory life (20)
وَتَذَرُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ (٢١)
and forsake the Hereafter. (21)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ (٢٢)
Some faces will be fresh on that day, (22)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ (٢٣)
looking to their Lord, (23)
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَاسِرَةٌ (٢٤)
and some faces will be scowling on that day, (24)
تَظُنُّ أَن يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ (٢٥)
knowing that they will be dealt out a punishment breaking the spine. (25)

Commentary

Radiant Faces and Scowling Faces on the Scene of Resurrection

In these verses, the discussion returns once again to the topics related to the Resurrection, describing further characteristics of that Day as well as the causes for denying the Resurrection. It states: "It is not that the proofs of the Resurrection are hidden such that you cannot realize its truth; rather, the reality is that you love this fleeting world" (كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ الْعاجِلَةَ).

"And for this reason, you abandon the Hereafter" (وَ تَذَرُونَ الْآخِرَةَ). The primary reason for denying the Resurrection is not doubt regarding the power of Allah or the gathering of "decayed bones" and scattered dust; rather, it is your intense attachment to the world, and rebellious lusts and desires that cause you to want to remove every obstacle and deterrent from your path. Since accepting the Resurrection and Divine commands and prohibitions creates numerous obstacles and limitations on this path, you rise to deny the core matter and abandon the Hereafter entirely.

As we have mentioned previously, one of the significant causes for the inclination toward materialism and the denial of the Origin and the Return is the pursuit of unconditional freedom in the face of lusts, pleasures, and every kind of sin. This is true not only in the past but even more evidently in today's world.

These two verses are, in fact, an emphasis on what was mentioned in the preceding verses: "But man desires to continue in sin ahead of him—He asks, 'When is the Day of Resurrection?'" [^1]

[^1]: Some have interpreted "Kalla" (Nay/By no means) in the above verses as a negation of their reflection upon the Great Quran, but this interpretation is not correct. As we have stated, in the verses related to the Quran, the addressee is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and it serves as a parenthetical point, whereas the verses under discussion are a continuation of the previous verses concerning the Resurrection.

Then, describing the state of the righteous believers and the wicked disbelievers on that Day, it says: "[Some] faces, that Day, will be radiant" (وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ ناضِرَةٌ). "Nadirah" (ناضرة) is derived from the root "Nadrah" (نضرة), meaning a specific freshness and glow that comes to a person due to an abundance of blessings and well-being, accompanied by joy, beauty, and luminosity. That is, the color of their faces reveals their state—how they are drowned in Divine blessings. In fact, this is similar to what appears in Surah Al-Mutaffifin, verse 24: "You will recognize in their faces the radiance of bliss" (تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ).

This pertains to material rewards; as for their spiritual rewards, He says: "Looking toward their Lord" (إِلى‌ رَبِّها ناظِرَةٌ). A looking with the eye of the heart and through inner intuition—a gaze that makes them captivated by that Peerless Essence and that Absolute Perfection and Beauty, granting them an indescribable spiritual pleasure, a single moment of which is superior to and higher than the entire world and all that is in it! Note that the advancement of "toward their Lord" (إِلى‌ رَبِّها) before "looking" (ناظِرَةٌ) signifies exclusivity, meaning they look only to Him and none other.

And if it is asked: "The inhabitants of Paradise will surely look at other than Him as well," we say: If they look at other than Him, they see everything as His signs, and looking at the effect is looking at the Producer. In other words, they see Him everywhere and behold His power, majesty, and beauty in everything. Therefore, attending to the blessings of Paradise does not distract them from gazing toward the Essence of Allah.

For this reason, in some narrations regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read: "They look toward the mercy of Allah, His blessing, and His reward," [^1] because looking at these is also looking at His Holy Essence.

A group of uninformed people have taken the above verse to refer to a sensory observation of Allah on the Day of Resurrection. They claim that on that Day, God will be seen with these very physical eyes! However, such an observation necessitates Allah being physical, existing in a place, and having a specific physical quality and state, whereas we know His Holy Essence is free from such taints, as emphasized repeatedly in various verses of the Quran. For instance, in Surah Al-An'am, verse 103, we read: "Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives [all] vision" (لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَ هُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارُ). This verse is absolute and is not restricted to this world.

In any case, the impossibility of a sensory observation of Allah is more obvious than the need for further discussion, and anyone with the slightest familiarity with the Quran and Islamic teachings will confess to this truth.

[^1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, pp. 464 and 465.

Some have mentioned another interpretation for "Nadirah" (ناظرة), saying it is derived from the root "Intidhar" (waiting/expecting). That is, the believers on that Day have their expectations only from the Holy Essence of God; they do not even rely on their own good deeds and are constantly awaiting His mercy and blessing.

And if it is said that such waiting would be mixed with a kind of anxiety, whereas there is no anxiety for the believers there, the answer is: The waiting mixed with anxiety is that which lacks certainty regarding its outcome. However, with certainty, such waiting is mixed with tranquility. [^1] Combining the meanings of "looking" and "expecting" does not seem far-fetched either, as using a single word for multiple meanings is permissible. However, if one of these two meanings must be intended, preference lies with the first meaning.

We conclude this discussion with a meaningful Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): He said: "When the people of Paradise enter Paradise, Allah the Exalted says: 'Do you desire anything more that I may give you?' They say: 'Have You not whitened our faces? Have You not entered us into Paradise and saved us from the Fire?'"

(Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 25, p. 304)

"He said: Then Allah the Exalted removes the veil, and they are given nothing more beloved to them than looking toward their Lord!"

"When the people of Paradise enter Paradise, Allah says: Do you want anything else for Me to add for you?

[^1]: Some believe that "Nadhar" (نظر) in the sense of "waiting" (انتظار) does not become transitive with "ila" (إلى), but rather without a preposition. However, there are evidences from Arabic poetry showing that "Nadhar" in the sense of "waiting" can also be transitive with "ila" (Refer to Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 398 and Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 6900).

They say: (O Lord, You have given us everything) Have You not whitened our faces? Have You not entered us into Paradise? And granted us salvation from the Fire? At that moment, the veils are removed (and they behold Allah with the eye of the heart), and in that state, nothing is more beloved to them than looking at their Lord." [^1] Interestingly, in a Hadith from Anas bin Malik from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it is narrated that he said: "They look toward their Lord without 'how' (quality), without a defined limit, and without a known attribute!" [^2] And this Hadith is an emphasis on inner intuition, not observation with the eyes.

And in contrast to this group of believers, there is a group whose faces are scowling and contorted: "And [some] faces, that Day, will be contorted" (وَ وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ باسِرَةٌ). "Basirah" (باسرة) is from the root "Basr" (بسر), meaning something unripe or an action before its time. Hence, the unripe fruit of the date palm is called "Busr," and subsequently, it is applied to the contorting of the face and scowling, because it is a reaction a person shows before the arrival of pain, punishment, and distress. In any case, when they behold the signs of punishment and see their records of deeds empty of virtues and full of vices, they become severely distressed, sorrowful, and grieved, and they contort their faces.

[^1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 29, p. 145. [^2]: "Tafsir al-Mizan," Vol. 20, p. 204.

"They know that a back-breaking punishment will be dealt to them" (تَظُنُّ أَنْ يُفْعَلَ بِها فاقِرَةٌ).

Many commentators believe that "Dhann" (ظن) here means "knowledge" (certainty); that is, they become certain of such a punishment. Meanwhile, some have said "Dhann" here retains its original meaning of "expectation" or "thought." Of course, they are certain in general that they will be punished, but they have an expectation regarding such a back-breaking punishment. [^1] "Faqirah" (فاقرة) is from the root "Faqrah" (فقرة), the plural of which is "Faqar" (فقار), meaning the vertebrae of the back. Therefore, "Faqirah" refers to a heavy calamity that breaks the vertebrae of the back. A "Faqir" (poor person) is called such as if his back is broken. [^2] In any case, this expression is a metaphor for the types of heavy punishments awaiting this group in Hell. This group expects back-breaking punishments, while the former group expects the mercy of the Lord and is ready for the meeting with the Beloved. These have the worst punishment, and those have the highest material blessings and spiritual gifts and pleasures.

[^1]: Among the evidences cited for this is that if "Dhann" meant "knowledge," the "an" (أن) following it should be "mukhaffafah" (lightened from the heavy form), whereas the "an" in the verse under discussion is "masdariyyah" (infinitive), evidenced by the fact that it has governed the subjunctive case (nasb). [^2]: "Faqirah" is an adjective for an omitted noun, understood as "dahiyah faqirah" (a back-breaking calamity). "Tadhunnu" is a verb whose subject is "wujuh" (faces), understood as "arbab al-wujuh" (the owners of the faces).

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلتَّرَاقِىَ (٢٦)
No indeed! When it reaches up to the collar bones (26)
وَقِيلَ مَنْ ۜ رَاقٍ (٢٧)
and it is said, ‘Who will take him up?’ (27)
وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ٱلْفِرَاقُ (٢٨)
and he knows that it is the [time of] parting, (28)
وَٱلْتَفَّتِ ٱلسَّاقُ بِٱلسَّاقِ (٢٩)
and each shank clasps the other shank, (29)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمَسَاقُ (٣٠)
that day he shall be driven toward your Lord. (30)

Commentary

In continuation of the discussions regarding the Hereafter and the fate of the believers and the disbelievers, these verses speak of the painful moment of death, which is the gateway to the other world.

It says: "No indeed! He will never believe until the soul reaches the collarbones" (كَلَّا إِذا بَلَغَتِ التَّراقِيَ) [^1]. It is on that day that his purgatorial (barzakhi) eye is opened, the veils are cast aside, he sees the signs of punishment and retribution, and becomes aware of his deeds. At that moment, he believes, but it is a faith that will never be of benefit to him.

"Taraqi" (تراقى) is the plural of "Tarquwah" (ترقوه), referring to the bones surrounding the throat (the collarbones). The soul reaching the throat is a metaphor for the final moments of life; for when the spirit leaves the body, the limbs furthest from the heart (like the hands and feet) cease to function first, as if the spirit is gradually withdrawing itself from the body until it reaches the throat.

At this moment, those around him, frantic and distressed, search for a way of salvation: "And it is said: 'Is there any healer who can come and save this patient from death?'" (وَ قِيلَ مَنْ راقٍ). They say this out of helplessness, despair, and misery, while they know that the matter is settled and even a physician can do nothing.

"Raq" (راق) is derived from the root "Raqy" (رقى) and "Ruqyah" (رقيه), meaning "to ascend." This term (Ruqyah) has been applied to incantations and prayers that lead to a patient's recovery. A physician is also called a "Raqi" because he delivers and saves the patient. Therefore, the meaning of the verse is that those around the patient—and sometimes the patient himself—cry out in intense distress: "Is there a doctor to be found?"

[^1]: "Idha" (إِذا) here is conditional and its result (jaza) is omitted; it is implied as: "When it reaches the collarbones, the reality of the matter is revealed to him, and he finds what he has done." Additionally, the subject of "balaghat" (بلغت) is "nafs" (the soul), which is omitted but understood from the context.

"Is there anyone who can recite a prayer so that this patient may be delivered?!" Some have also said the meaning of the verse is: "Which of the angels will seize his soul and take it up? The angels of punishment or the angels of mercy?" Some add that since the angels of Allah loathe taking and ascending with the soul of such a faithless person, the Angel of Death says: "Who is it that will take his soul and carry it up?"

However, the first commentary is the most correct and appropriate of all.

In the next verse, it refers to the complete despair of the "Muhtadar" (the person in the throes of death), saying: "At this state, he becomes absolutely despondent of life and becomes certain of the parting and separation from the world" (وَ ظَنَّ أَنَّهُ الْفِراقُ).

"And one leg is twisted with the other leg," and the moment of death arrives (وَ الْتَفَّتِ السَّاقُ بِالسَّاقِ). This twisting is either due to the intense agony of the soul departing, or as a result of the limbs ceasing to function and the spirit being withdrawn from them.

Other interpretations have also been narrated for this verse. Among them, in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The world is entwined with the Hereafter" [^1]. The same meaning appears in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim [^2].

[^1] and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 465.

It is also narrated from Ibn Abbas that it means "the intertwining of the severity of the matter of the Hereafter with the matter of the world."

Some have also said: It refers to the intertwining of the hardships of death with the hardships of the Resurrection.

It appears that all of these return to the same meaning narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام. This interpretation is derived from the fact that one of the meanings of "Saq" (ساق) in the Arabic language is a severe event, a calamity, or a great affliction.

Some have also said: It refers to the wrapping of the legs together in the shroud (kafan).

Of course, these interpretations are not in conflict with one another, and all of them can be encompassed within the meaning of the verse.

In the final verse under discussion, He says: "The journey of all creation on that day is toward the court of your Lord" (إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمَساقُ). Yes, everyone returns to Him, appearing in His court of justice, and all paths shall end with Him.

This verse is both an emphasis on the issue of the Resurrection and the general rising of the servants, and it can also be an allusion to the direction of the evolutionary movement of creation toward His Holy Essence, which is an infinite Essence in every respect.

Key Point: The Painful Moment of Death

As we know, the Quran repeatedly emphasizes the issue of death and specifically the moment of the soul departing, warning human beings that everyone has such a moment ahead. Sometimes it is expressed as "Sakrat al-Mawt" (the stupor and dizziness of death) [^1], sometimes as "Ghamarat al-Mawt" meaning "the agonies of death" [^2], sometimes as "the soul reaching the throat" [^3], and sometimes as "the soul reaching the collarbones" (the verses under discussion). From the sum of these, it is clearly understood that—contrary to what some materialists say—that moment is a difficult and painful one. Why should it not be so, when it is the moment of transition from this world to another? Just as the transition of a human from the womb to this world is accompanied by great pain and suffering, the transition to the next world will naturally be the same.

However, it is understood from Islamic narrations that this moment passes easily for true believers, while it is intensely painful for the faithless. This is because the longing to meet the Lord and His infinite mercy and blessings

[^1]: "And the stupor of death brings the truth" (Surah Qaf, Verse 19).

[^2]: Surah Al-An'am, 93.

[^3]: "Then why, when it [the soul] reaches the throat" (Al-Waqi'ah, 83).

so overwhelms the first group that they do not feel the pains of the moment of transition.

In contrast, for the second group, the terror of punishment on one hand, and the tragedy of parting from the world they were attached to on the other, doubles the pains of the moment of transition from the world.

In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about death, he said: "For the believer, death is like taking off a filthy, insect-ridden garment, and undoing heavy shackles and chains, and replacing them with the finest clothes, the most fragrant perfumes, the most comfortable mounts, and the most peaceful homes. For the disbeliever, it is like taking off a magnificent garment and moving from comfortable homes, and replacing them with the filthiest and coarsest clothes, the most terrifying dwellings, and the greatest punishment" [^1]. In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that a person asked him for a description of death. The Imam said:

"For the believer, it is like the most pleasant fragrance he smells, whereby he falls into a slumber due to its pleasantness, and all exhaustion and pain are severed from him. For the disbeliever, it is like the sting of vipers and the bite of scorpions, or even more severe!" [^2]. In any case, death is the gateway to the realm of eternity, as stated in a Hadith from Ali عليه السلام:

"Every house has a door, and the door to the house of the Hereafter is death" [^1]. Indeed, mindfulness of it has a profound effect on breaking desires, ending long-term worldly ambitions, and cleansing the rust of negligence from the mirror of the heart. Thus, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Remembrance of death kills the rebellious desires within the soul, uproots the seeds of negligence, strengthens the heart with Allah's promises, softens one's nature, breaks the banners of caprice, extinguishes the fire of greed, and makes the world seem small. This is the meaning of what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'An hour of reflection is better than a year of worship!'" [^2]. Of course, the intention is to state one of the clear instances of reflection, not that the subject of reflection is limited to it.

A further discussion on this matter was presented in Volume 22, page 235 (under Verse 19 of Surah Qaf).

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 155.

[^2]: Ibid, p. 152.

[^1]: Sharh Nahj al-Balagha by Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 20, p. 345.

[^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 133.

Ayahs

فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّىٰ (٣١)
He neither confirmed [the messages of Allah], nor did he pray, (31)
وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (٣٢)
but denied [them] and turned away, (32)
ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ يَتَمَطَّىٰٓ (٣٣)
and went back swaggering to his family. (33)
أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰ (٣٤)
So, woe to you! Woe to you! (34)
ثُمَّ أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰٓ (٣٥)
Again, woe to you! Woe to you! (35)
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى (٣٦)
Does man suppose that he has been abandoned to futility? (36)
أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِىٍّ يُمْنَىٰ (٣٧)
Was he not a drop of emitted semen, (37)
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ (٣٨)
and then a clinging mass? Whereat He created and proportioned [him], (38)
فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ (٣٩)
and made of him the two sexes, male and female. (39)
أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ (٤٠)
Is not [someone like] that able to revive the dead? (40)

Commentary

The God Who Created Man from a Lowly Drop...

In continuation of the discussions regarding "death"—which is the first step in the journey to the Hereafter and was mentioned in the preceding verses—the verses under discussion speak of the empty-handedness of the disbelievers regarding the provisions for this journey.

First, It says: "This man who denies the Resurrection never believed, nor did he attest to the truth of God's signs, nor did he offer prayer" (فَلا صَدَّقَ وَ لا صَلَّى) [1].

"Rather, he took the path of denial and turned his back on Allah's command (وَ لكِنْ كَذَّبَ وَ تَوَلَّى)." The meaning of the phrase fa-la saddaqa (he did not attest to the truth) is the lack of affirmation regarding the Day of Judgment, the Reckoning, the Reward, the Divine signs,

[1]: The pronoun in the verbs "saddaqa" and "salla" refers to the "man who denies the Resurrection," which is understood from the tone of the speech and was also alluded to at the beginning of the Surah.

Monotheism, and the Prophethood of the Prophet of Islam ﷺ. However, some have considered it an allusion to the abandonment of spending and "charity" (sadaqah) by the disbelievers, due to its mention alongside prayer.

Yet, the second verse clearly testifies that the opposite of this "attestation" is "denial" (takdhib); therefore, the first interpretation seems more accurate.

In the next verse, It adds: "Then he went to his family, walking arrogantly" (ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِ يَتَمَطَّى). Imagining that he had achieved a great victory by disregarding and denying the Prophet ﷺ and the Divine signs, he was intoxicated with the wine of pride. He would go to his family to recount, as usual, the "boastful" events that occurred outside the home; even his gait and the movement of his limbs expressed this haughtiness and vanity.

Yatamatta is derived from the root matat, which originally means "back," and tamatti means stretching the back out of indifference and pride, or lethargy and listlessness. Here, the first meaning is intended.

Some also consider it to be from the root matt (rhyming with khatt), meaning to stretch the legs or other limbs when showing indifference or laziness, but its derivation from matat is more consistent with the literal form [1]. In any case, this meaning is similar to what appears in verse 31 of Surah al-Mutaffifin:

[1]: Because if it is from the root matat, no change occurs in the literal form, whereas if it were from the root matt, the word yatamatta would have originally been yatamattat, where the final ta was changed to a ya.

"And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting" (وَ إِذَا انْقَلَبُوا إِلى‌ أَهْلِهِمُ انْقَلَبُوا فَكِهِينَ)—meaning they would speak mockingly about the believers.

Then, addressing such faithless individuals as a threat, It says: "Woe to you, and more woe!" (أَوْلى‌ لَكَ فَأَوْلى‌).

"Again, woe to you, and more woe!" (ثُمَّ أَوْلى‌ لَكَ فَأَوْلى‌). Several other interpretations have been mentioned for this verse, including:

  • It is a threat meaning: "May punishment be upon you, and again punishment be upon you."
  • Or: "This state you are in is most deserving of you, most deserving."
  • Or: "Reproach and blame are better for you, and again better."
  • Or: "Woe unto you, and again woe unto you."
  • Or: "May the good of this world be far from you, and the good of the Hereafter also be far."
  • Or: "May evil and punishment overtake you, and again may evil and punishment overtake you."
  • Or: "The punishment you witness on the field of Badr is more deserving for you in this world, and the punishment of the grave and Resurrection is also more deserving for you" [1].

[1]: According to some interpretations, awla here is a "superlative noun" (af'al al-tafdil), and according to others, awla is a "past tense verb" from the if'al form of the root waliya, and the meaning of the sentence becomes "Allah has brought the punishment near to you." Some have said awla is a "verbal noun" (ism fi'l) meaning "it has drawn near," but the first meaning is most appropriate (refer to Al-Bayan fi Gharib I'rab al-Quran, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Al-Mizan, and Al-Munjid).

However, it is evident that most of these meanings return to one general and comprehensive concept, which encompasses the threat of punishment, blame, evil, and retribution, whether it be the punishment of this world, the Barzakh (purgatory), or the Resurrection.

It is narrated that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ took Abu Jahl by the hand (and according to some narrations, by his collar) and said: "أَوْلى‌ لَكَ فَأَوْلى‌ ثُمَّ أَوْلى‌ لَكَ فَأَوْلى‌". Abu Jahl replied: "With what do you threaten me? Neither you nor your Lord can cause me any harm; I am the most powerful person in this land!" It was then that these very phrases were revealed to the Prophet ﷺ as Quranic verses [1].

Then, the text proceeds to two interesting arguments regarding the Resurrection: one through the explanation of the "purpose of creation and the wisdom of Allah," and the other through the explanation of His power based on the transformation and evolution of the human embryo in various stages of the womb. In the first stage, It says: "Does man think that he will be left neglected [without purpose]?" (أَ يَحْسَبُ الْإِنْسانُ أَنْ يُتْرَكَ سُدىً).

Suda (rhyming with huda) means futile, vain, and aimless. The Arabs say "a camel is suda" regarding a camel left without a shepherd, grazing wherever it wishes.

The "man" referred to in this verse is the same man who denies the Resurrection. The verse asks: How can he believe that Allah created this vast universe, with all its grandeur and wonders, for man, yet there is no purpose in man's creation? How can one believe that every organ of

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 401.

man was created for a specific purpose—the eye for seeing, the ear for hearing, and the heart for delivering food, oxygen, and water to all the cells of the body—even the lines on a person's fingertips have a wisdom, yet there is no purpose for his entire existence? Was he created in vain, neglected, without any program, command, prohibition, duty, or responsibility?

If an ordinary person makes a small object without a purpose, he is criticized and removed from the ranks of rational people. How could Allah, the Absolutely Wise, have such a purposeless creation?! And if it is said that the goal is merely this few-day life of the world—this repetitive eating and sleeping mixed with a thousand kinds of pain and suffering—this is certainly not something that could justify that great creation.

Therefore, we conclude that this human was created for a greater goal, namely, eternal life in the proximity of Divine Mercy and continuous, endless perfection [1].

Then, stating the second reason, It adds: "Was he not a drop of sperm emitted [into the womb]?" (أَ لَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِنْ مَنِيٍّ يُمْنى‌).

"Then he passed this stage and became a clinging clot ('alaqah),

[1]: We had another discussion on this topic under verse 115 of Surah al-Mu'minun (Vol. 14, p. 346).

and Allah gave him a new creation and proportioned him" (ثُمَّ كانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّى‌).

Again, he did not stop at this stage: "And He made of him two mates, the male and the female" (فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَ الْأُنْثى‌).

Is the One who grants a new creation every day to a small, lowly drop in the darkness of the mother's womb, clothing it in a new garment of life and giving it a new form until it finally becomes a complete male or female human and is born—"Is such a One not able to bring the dead to life?" (أَ لَيْسَ ذلِكَ بِقادِرٍ عَلى‌ أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتى‌). This statement is, in fact, a response to the deniers who often spoke of the impossibility of the physical Resurrection and denied the possibility of returning to life after death and becoming dust. The Quran, to prove the possibility of this concept, takes man by the hand and returns him to the beginning of his creation, showing him the amazing stages of the embryo and the wonderful transformations of man during these stages, so that he may know that He is Capable and Powerful over all things. In other words, the best proof for the possibility of a thing is its occurrence.

Key Points

1. Embryonic Transformations or Repeated Resurrections!

Nutfah originally means a small amount of water or pure water; subsequently, it was applied to the drops of fluid that, through fertilization, cause the emergence of a human or an animal.

In truth, the transformation of the nutfah during the embryonic period is among the most amazing phenomena of the universe, which is the subject of the science of "Embryology." In recent centuries, the veil has been lifted from its secrets to a great extent.

The Quran, in an era when these matters were not yet discovered, repeatedly emphasized it as one of the signs of Allah's power, and this itself is one of the signs of the greatness of this magnificent Heavenly Book.

Although only some embryonic stages are mentioned in these verses, more stages are explained in other verses of the Quran, such as the verses at the beginning of Surah al-Hajj and the beginning of Surah al-Mu'minun, and we have provided further explanation in this regard under those verses [1]. Additionally, the use of dhalika (that), which is a demonstrative pronoun for the distant, in reference to Allah is an allusion to the greatness of His station and an indication that His Pure Essence is so exalted that It is beyond the reach of human thoughts.

It is mentioned in a narration that when the verse "Is such a One not able to bring the dead to life?" (أَ لَيْسَ ذلِكَ بِقادِرٍ عَلى‌ أَنْ يُحْيِيَ الْمَوْتى‌) was revealed, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ submitted: "Glory be to You, O Allah, and yes! [You are]."

The same has been narrated from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [2].

[1]: Refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 14, from p. 17 onwards and from p. 206 onwards. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 402.

2. The System of Gender in the Human World

Despite all the discussions regarding the factors of the embryo's gender and what influences its becoming "male" or "female," no one yet knows exactly what the primary factors are.

It is true that certain foods or some medications might not be without influence in this matter, but certainly, none are considered the determining factor. In other words, this is a matter whose knowledge is with Allah the All-Knowing.

On the other hand, a relative balance is always seen between these two genders in all societies. Although in most societies the number of women is slightly higher, and rarely in some societies the number of men is slightly higher, overall, a relative balance exists between these two genders.

If, hypothetically, a day were to come when this balance was disrupted—for example, if the number of women became ten times that of men, or the number of men ten times that of women—think how the system of human society would be disrupted? What strange corruptions would arise from this, where for every one woman there are ten men, or for every ten men there is one woman, and what an uproar would ensue!

The above verses which say, "And He made of him two mates, the male and the female" (فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَ الْأُنْثى‌), are a subtle and concise allusion to these two subjects: on one hand, they point to the mysterious diversity of humans and their division into these two genders during the embryonic period, and on the other hand, to this relative balance [1].

[1]: The common saying that the number of women in every society is greater than men—which is taken as one of the reasons for polygamy—is acceptable, but this does not contradict the relative balance. For example, in a society of 50 million people, there may be 26 million women and 24 million men, meaning the difference is only about one-tenth or less. However, the number of women being several times that of men has never been seen in any society.

O Allah! We bear witness that You are Capable of clothing all the dead in the garment of life in a single moment; nothing is difficult or complex before Your power. O Lord! On that day when the souls reach the throats and we lose hope in everything, our only hope is in Your Pure Essence. O God! Acquaint us with the purpose of creation.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Qiyamah. 7th of Rabi' al-Awwal, 1407.
